question_title: Will the interest in “kelsey fitzsimmons” change between 2026-03-30 and 2026-04-10 according to Google Trends?
summary: The Kelsey Fitzsimmons case peaked on Google Trends on Mar 26 (acquittal verdict), with values decaying rapidly: 100→50→21→12→9 over 4 days. The trial is fully concluded with no scheduled follow-up events. An exponential decay model projects a value of ~3 by Apr 10, well below the starting value of 9. The pre-spike baseline averaged 3.7 (mostly 0-5 range). Since the "Decreases" threshold requires the Apr 10 value to be <6, and baseline reversion strongly favors this outcome, "Decreases" is the most likely resolution. "Doesn't change" (value 6-12) remains possible due to measurement noise (SerpAPI vs pytrends ±1-3 points) or minor follow-up news. "Increases" (>12) would require a significant new development landing near Apr 10.

factors.description: Post-trial decay pattern: verdict was Mar 26 (peak 100), values decayed to 9 by Mar 30. Exponential decay model projects ~3 by Apr 10, well below the threshold of 6 for 'Decreases'.
Pre-spike baseline was ~3.7 (range 0-10). By Apr 10 (15 days post-verdict), interest should return to this baseline. Most baseline days had values 0-5, clearly below the ≤6 threshold for 'Decreases'.

Trial is fully concluded (acquitted Mar 26). No scheduled follow-up events found (no appeal, no civil suit filed yet, no hearings). She said 'my fight is not over' but nothing concrete is scheduled in the Apr 1-10 window.

SerpAPI measurement noise (±1-3 points vs pytrends) creates uncertainty near thresholds. At projected value ~3, a +3 measurement shift would put it at 6 (barely 'Doesn't change'). This uncertainty favors keeping meaningful probability on 'Doesn't change'.

Possible follow-up news (civil lawsuit filing, media interview, police reform discussion) could generate a modest bump in the 7-15 range on a specific day. The related queries show 'body cams' and 'liability' as active subtopics.
A major new development (unexpected civil suit, major media appearance, new incident) could spike interest above 12 on Apr 10 specifically. Very unlikely to hit exactly on Apr 10.

reasoning:
**Forecast: Decreases (66%), Doesn't change (28%), Increases (6%)**

**Key reasoning:**

- **The story is over.** Kelsey Fitzsimmons (former North Andover police officer) was acquitted of assault on Mar 26, producing a massive peak (100) on Google Trends. By Mar 30, interest had already decayed to 9.

- **Decay to baseline is the dominant path.** The pre-spike baseline averaged ~3.7 (mostly 0-5). An exponential decay fit projects ~3 by Apr 10 — clearly below the threshold of 6 needed for "Decreases" to resolve.

- **Threshold arithmetic favors "Decreases."** Starting at 9, "Doesn't change" requires the Apr 10 value to stay in [6, 12]. With no scheduled events and the trial concluded, sustaining a value of 6+ for 11 more days is unlikely but not impossible if follow-up news or measurement noise keeps values slightly elevated.

- **"Increases" is improbable** — it would require a value >12, meaning a significant new development landing on or near Apr 10 specifically.
resolution_criteria: This question resolves “Doesn't change” if the value on the timeline at [https://trends.google.com/trends/explore?geo=US&tz=0&q=kelsey%20fitzsimmons&date=2026-03-11%202026-04-10](https://trends.google.com/trends/explore?geo=US&tz=0&q=kelsey%20fitzsimmons&date=2026-03-11%202026-04-10) for 2026-04-10 is within 3 of the value at 2026-03-30. It resolves “Increases” if the value is more than 3 greater, and resolves “Decreases” if the value is more than 3 lower.
fine_print:
A script will be used to determine the resolution of this question. It will access the data on Google Trends using [SerpApi](https://serpapi.com/), and compare the height of the timeline for the two aforementioned dates. The specific python query will be structured as follows:
```
from serpapi import GoogleSearch

search = GoogleSearch(params={
  "api_key": API_KEY,
  "engine": "google_trends",
  "data_type": "TIMESERIES",
  "geo": "US",
  "tz": 0,
  "q": "kelsey fitzsimmons",
  "date": "2026-03-11 2026-04-10",
})
search.get_dict()
```
Note that there may be differences between the results returned by the API and the data appearing on the page. This seems to be due to the 'tz' parameter not having the intended effect in-browser. In this case, the API results will be considered authoritative.
Note that the precision of the timeline will be 1 day, so this will compare the overall interest for the whole day as determined by Google Trends.
Dates are determined in UTC.
If the data is no longer available, or the script fails, this question will be annulled or manually resolved by a moderator.
